title: The do-while Loop 🔄
description: A loop that guarantees at least one execution.

slides.title: Act First, Ask Later 🛑
slides.content:
A standard `while` loop checks its condition at the very top. If the condition is false immediately, the loop code inside **never runs**.

But what if you want the code to run *at least once*, no matter what?

slides.title: The do-while Syntax
slides.content:
A `do-while` loop flips the structure. It puts the code block first (`do`), and the `while` condition at the very bottom.

*Notice the semicolon `;` required at the end!*

quizData.question: What is the primary feature of a `do-while` loop compared to a regular `while` loop?

options.text: It guarantees the code block runs at least one time.

quizData.explanation: Because the condition test happens at the bottom of the block, the computer must run through the code at least once before hitting the test.

slides.title: When is it useful? 🤔
slides.content:
The `do-while` loop is perfect for user menus.

Imagine an ATM. You want to show the menu and ask for input *at least once*. Then, if they press "Quit", the condition becomes false and the loop ends.

quizData.question: Unlike a normal `while` loop, a `do-while` loop must end with a semicolon `;` after the condition.
quizData.explanation: Correct! It is written as `do { ... } while (condition);`. Forgetting the trailing semicolon is a common syntax error.
